text.tspan: WonderDrive · full answer + visual evidence + always-visible directions
Rough Excalidraw composition · the answer, selected image, and image notes live together inside a fixed viewport
Can a public space teach people how to behave?
A public space teaches mostly through hints, not lectures. A wide
sidewalk invites people to walk side by side; shade and a comfortable
bench say that staying is welcome. [15]
Designers call these cues affordances: features that make an action
easy, visible, or socially natural. People also learn by watching how
other people use the same place. [21]
But these lessons are not neutral. Fences, hostile seating, lighting,
and narrow gates can quietly communicate who may enter or linger.
The behavior of a place emerges from design, observation, and habit.
It shows seating, shade, and circulation
working together in one public space.
• Curved benches form shared and private zones
Can a bench make strangers talk to each other?
Who decides where people are allowed to linger?
The full answer, selected image, and teaching
